region_of_origin: Singapore / Southeast Asia (Chinese Hakka and Cantonese origin)
food_subgroup: Herbal plant-based jelly with rock sugar syrup; served cold as a standalone dessert or hawker topping

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn URI exists for Grass Jelly / Xiancao (Platostoma palustre jelly). Closest parent would be a generic plant-based gel dessert. Null retained pending FoodOn expansion.
ontology.foodex2_description: Chinese herbal jelly (Platostoma palustre / Mesona chinensis) — closest parent match in FoodEx2
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: A0GBW is the closest defensible FoodEx2 anchor; no exact code for Xiancao grass jelly exists in the current FoodEx2 catalogue.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for Platostoma palustre jelly or grass jelly dessert.

serving.description: 1 bowl (~200g grass jelly strips + 20ml rock sugar syrup, ~220g total)
serving.natural_language_portion: One standard hawker-centre serving is approximately one bowl containing 200g of grass jelly strips topped with 20ml of rock sugar syrup, totalling approximately 220g and providing around 86 kcal.

nutrient_source.title: Grass Jelly — Health Promotion Board Singapore (Foodo / HPB Nutrient Composition Database)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values per 100g sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for grass jelly (Xiancao) served with rock sugar syrup. Values represent the combined jelly-plus-syrup product as typically served. Confidence: high.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of grass jelly dessert with rock sugar syrup — analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database entries for plant-based gel desserts and sucrose-sweetened beverages
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for Xiancao grass jelly exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI ~45 (LOW) is an analogous estimate: the jelly component alone has a GI of approximately 5 (near-zero carbohydrate, starch-set gel with negligible glycaemic effect); the rock sugar syrup component contributes sucrose with GI ~65. The blended GI of ~45 for the combined served product is consistent with the proportion of syrup in the standard 220g serving and is supported by the HPB Singapore clinical assessment. GL per serving = (GI × available carbohydrate per serving) / 100 = (45 × 17.6g) / 100 ≈ 7.9. Confidence: 0.9.

hypertension.key_factor: Negligible sodium (~11mg per 220g serving) and zero fat
hypertension.guidance: Grass jelly with standard rock sugar syrup provides approximately 11mg sodium per bowl, which is nutritionally negligible and poses no concern for blood pressure management in hypertensive individuals.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycaemic load (~7.9 per serving) with option to reduce syrup
type2_diabetes.guidance: With a GL of approximately 7.9 per standard serving, grass jelly is one of the most suitable hawker desserts for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes; requesting reduced or no rock sugar syrup at the stall reduces GL to near-zero, as the jelly itself contributes only ~3g carbohydrate per bowl.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Zero saturated fat, zero cholesterol, and very low calorie density
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Grass jelly contains no saturated fat, no cholesterol, and negligible sodium, making it a cardiovascularly neutral dessert choice; the Platostoma palustre herb contains rosmarinic acid, a polyphenol with potential anti-inflammatory properties, though clinical evidence at culinary doses remains limited.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Grass Jelly (Xiancao / 仙草 / Cincau Hitam) is a traditional Chinese and Malay herbal cold dessert made from the dried aerial parts of Platostoma palustre (formerly Mesona chinensis), a mint-family herb. The dried herb is boiled for several hours with water and a small amount of rice starch or tapioca starch, then cooled and set into a characteristic black, mildly bitter, smooth jelly. It is served cold in strips or cubes, typically topped with rock sugar syrup, and optionally garnished with red bean, grass jelly pearls, or fresh fruit. In Singapore, it is sold at hawker centre dessert stalls, in canned form (e.g. Air Grass Jelly, F&N Grass Jelly Drink), and as a topping in ABC Ice / Ice Kachang. Per 100g of the served product (jelly + syrup): 39 kcal, 0.5g protein, 0g fat, 8g carbohydrate (all as sugar from syrup), 0.5g dietary fibre, 5mg sodium. Per standard bowl (~220g): approximately 86 kcal, GL ~7.9 (LOW). The jelly alone without syrup provides only ~12 kcal per bowl with a GI of approximately 5 — essentially calorie-free. This is NOT the same as Guilinggao (龜苓膏), which is made from smilax root and turtle herb and has a distinctly different herb profile and clinical context. Grass jelly is suitable for vegetarians, vegans, and halal diets. It is one of the most diabetes-friendly hawker desserts available in Singapore. Source: HPB Singapore (Foodo). Confidence: high.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database, the primary approved source for Singaporean hawker foods. The food is a well-documented, widely consumed Singapore hawker dessert with stable preparation. GI is an analogous estimate (confidence 0.9) as no direct GI measurement exists in the University of Sydney GI Database for this specific product.
notes: fat_g is confirmed zero per HPB Singapore Foodo: grass jelly is a starch-set herbal gel with no added fat; fat is naturally zero by product composition. cholesterol_mg is confirmed zero consistent with a plant-based, fat-free product. monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions not reported in HPB Singapore Foodo database for this product. water_g set to -1: moisture content not separately reported in the HPB Singapore source for this product. trans_fat_g reported as 0.0 per HPB Singapore source (confirmed zero). zinc_mg and selenium_mcg reported as 0.0 (confirmed trace/below detection threshold per HPB Singapore source). All vitamins reported as 0.0 except folate (1.0 mcg/100g) — confirmed per HPB Singapore source; vitamin values are trace or below detection threshold for this herbal gel product. GI ~45 is an analogous estimate for the combined jelly-plus-syrup product; the jelly component alone has GI ~5. GL per serving calculated as (45 × 17.6g available carbohydrate per 220g serving) / 100 ≈ 7.9. This food is NOT the same as Guilinggao (龜苓膏). FoodOn URI is null: no exact FoodOn concept exists for Platostoma palustre jelly dessert as of April 2026.
